back_translation: When Jesus had finished speaking all these words, He said to His disciples,
"You all know that in two more days will be the Passover festival, and the Son of Man will be handed over to be crucified on a cross."
At that time, the chief priests and the elders of the people assembled at the courtyard of the high priest whose name was Caiaphas,
and they conferred together in order to arrest Jesus by stratagem and kill him.
But they said, "Let us not do it during the festival, lest there be unrest among the people."
While Jesus was staying at the village of Bethany in the house of Simon, the one who had been a leper,
a certain woman, holding an alabaster stone jar containing very expensive perfume, came to Him, and poured it on His head as He was reclining at the meal.
When the disciples saw this, they were displeased and said, "Why must there be such waste?
For this perfume could have been sold for a high price, and the money could have been given to the poor."
Jesus, knowing, said to them, "Why do you trouble this woman? She has done a good and beautiful thing toward me.
For the poor will always be with you, but I will not always be with you.
That she poured this perfume on my body, she did it to prepare for the burial of my body.
Truly I say to you, wherever this gospel is proclaimed throughout the whole world, what this woman has done will also be told in remembrance of her."
At that time, one of the twelve disciples, the one whose name was Judas Iscariot, went to the chief priests
and said, "What will you all give me if I hand him over to you?" So they counted out thirty pieces of silver for him.
From that time on, Judas kept looking for an opportunity to hand Him over.
On the first day of the Feast of Unleavened Bread, the disciples came to Jesus and asked, "Where do You desire for us to prepare the Passover meal for You?"
He said, "Go into the city to a certain man and say to him, 'The Teacher says, my time is drawing near. I will keep the Passover festival at your house with my disciples.'"
The disciples did as Jesus had commanded them, and they prepared the Passover meal.
When it was evening, He reclined at the meal together with the twelve disciples.
While they were eating, He said, "Truly I say to you, one of you will hand me over."
They were exceedingly grieved, and began to ask Him one by one, "It is not I, is it, my Lord?"
He answered, "The one who has dipped his hand in the same bowl with me, that is the one who will hand me over.

The Son of Man will depart just as it has been written about Him. But woe to that man through whose act the Son of Man is handed over. If that man had not been born, it would have been better for him."
Judas, who would hand Him over, answered, "It is not I, is it, Rabbi?" He said to him, "You yourself have said it."
While they were still eating, Jesus took bread, blessed, broke it, and gave it to the disciples, saying, "Take it; eat. This is my body."
Then He took a cup, gave thanks, and gave it to them, saying, "All of you, drink from this cup,
for this is my blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many so that they may receive the forgiveness of sins.
I say to you, I will not drink of this fruit of the vine anymore from now, until that day when I drink together with you anew in my Father's kingdom."
When they had sung songs of praise, they went out to the Mount of Olives.
Then Jesus said to them, "On this very night, all of you will stumble and fall because of me. For it is written, 'I will strike the shepherd, and the sheep of the flock will be scattered.'
But after I have risen, I will go ahead of you into the region of Galilee."
Peter answered Him, "Even if everyone stumbles and falls because of You, I will never stumble and fall."
Jesus said to him, "Truly I tell you, on this very night, before a rooster crows, you will deny me three times."
Peter said to Him, "Even if I must die together with You, I will never deny You." And all the disciples said the same thing.
Then Jesus came with them to a place named Gethsemane, and He said to the disciples, "Sit here while I go to pray over there."
He took Peter and the two sons of Zebedee with Him, and began to be grieved and deeply troubled.
Then He said to them, "My soul is sorrowful to the point of death. Stay here and keep watch with me."
He went forward a little, and prostrated His face down and prayed, "My Father, if it is possible, let this cup pass away from me. But nevertheless, let it not be as I will, but as Your will."
He came back to the disciples and found them sleeping, and said to Peter, "Could you all not watch with me for one hour?
Keep watching and pray, so that you will not fall into temptation. The spirit indeed is ready and willing, but the flesh is weak."
He went again a second time and prayed, "My Father, if this cup cannot pass away unless I drink it, then let it be according to Your will."
He came back again and found them sleeping, because their eyes were heavy.
So He left them, went again, and prayed a third time with the same words again.
Then He came to the disciples and said to them, "Are you still sleeping and resting? Behold, the time has drawn near, and the Son of Man is being handed over into the hands of sinners.
Rise up, let us go! Behold, the one who will hand me over has drawn near."

While He was still speaking, behold, Judas, who was one of the twelve disciples, arrived. And with him a large crowd carrying swords and clubs came, sent from the chief priests and the elders of the people.
Beforehand, the one who would hand Him over had agreed on a signal with them: "Whomever I kiss, that is the one. Arrest him."
He went directly to Jesus immediately and said, "Greetings, Rabbi!" and then kissed Him.
Jesus said to him, "Friend, do what you came for." Then they stepped in, laid hands on Jesus, and arrested Him.
Behold, one of those with Jesus stretched out his hand, drew his sword, struck the servant of the high priest, and cut off his ear.
Jesus said to him, "Put your sword back in its sheath as before. For everyone who uses the sword must die by the sword.
Or do you think that I cannot ask my Father, and He would send me more than twelve legions of angels this very moment?
If so, how would the Scriptures, which say that the event must happen this way, be fulfilled?"

At that moment, Jesus said to the crowd, "Have you come out to arrest me with swords and clubs as though I were a robber? I have sat teaching in the temple precinct every day, and you did not arrest me.
But all these events happened so that the words in the Scriptures of the prophets would be fulfilled." Then all the disciples abandoned Him and fled.
Those who had arrested Jesus led Him to the house of Caiaphas, the high priest, where the scribes and the elders had gathered.
But Peter followed Him at a distance, as far as the courtyard of the high priest. He went inside and sat with the officers to see how the event would end.
The chief priests and the whole Sanhedrin council tried to seek false testimony to accuse Jesus, in order to execute Him.
But they could not find any, even though many false witnesses came forward to testify. Finally, two came forward to testify
and said, "This man has said, 'I am able to destroy the temple of God and rebuild it in three days.'"
The high priest then stood up and said to Him, "Do You not answer anything? What are these men testifying against You?"
But Jesus remained silent. The high priest then said to Him, "I make you swear by the living God — tell us whether you are the Christ, the Son of God, or not."
Jesus said to him, "You yourself have said it. But I tell all of you, from now on you will see the Son of Man sitting at the right hand of the Mighty One, and coming with the clouds of heaven."
Then the high priest tore his garments and said, "He has blasphemed against God. Why do we still need witnesses? Behold, now you have heard the blasphemous words.
What do you all think?" They answered, "He deserves the punishment of death."
Then they spat into His face and struck Him. Some slapped His face
saying, "O Christ, prophesy to us! Who is the one who struck you?"
Meanwhile, Peter was sitting in the outer courtyard. A young servant girl came to him and said, "You also were with Jesus the Galilean."
But he denied it before all of them, saying, "I do not know what you are talking about."
When he went out to the gate, another servant girl saw him and said to the people standing there, "This one was with Jesus of Nazareth also."
So he denied it again, together with swearing an oath, saying, "I do not know that man."
After a little while, those who were standing there came over to Peter and said, "Certainly, you are one of them too, for the accent of your speech makes it clearly evident."
Then he began to curse himself and swear an oath, saying, "I do not know that man." Immediately, a rooster crowed.
Peter then remembered the word that Jesus had spoken: "Before a rooster crows, you will deny me three times." He went out outside and wept bitterly.
